data.title: A new way to use stablecoins: "Government bond cashback"
data.description: This could be the beginning of a shift in stablecoins from being "issuer-driven" to "payment network-driven." Open Standard, an independent entity comprised of over 140 financial and technology companies including Stripe, Visa, BlackRock, and Coinbase, announced the launch of its USD stablecoin project, Open USD (OUSD), with plans to complete on-chain deployment on networks such as Solana, Stellar, Base, and Polygon by the end of 2026. Zach Abrams, co-founder of Bridge, the stablecoin infrastructure company previously acquired by Stripe for $1.1 billion, will serve as the first CEO of Open Standard. From the perspective of the participants, OUSD's supporters cover payment networks, traditional banks, asset management institutions, crypto platforms, and large technology companies, bringing almost all the key players needed for stablecoins to enter the mainstream payment system into the same alliance. The project will be governed by a board of directors composed of its members, attempting to establish a completely new profit-sharing structure among issuance, distribution, and use cases. This initial alliance, which can be described as half of the traditional financial and crypto worlds, is based on the core logic of decentralizing the nominal issuer and shifting the trillion-dollar interest pool previously monopolized by Circle and Tether towards the distribution side that actually contributes transaction volume. Currently, Tether dominates the market with a circulating supply of USDT of $185 billion, while Circle holds second place with a circulating supply of USDC of approximately $75 billion. The interest income from their reserves constitutes the main body of this interest pool. The design mechanism of OUSD can be summarized in three points: zero-cost minting and redemption, returning reserve returns to partners after deducting management expenses, and a board of directors jointly governed by partners. As the nominal issuer, Open Standard does not retain the main interest income. Instead, it distributes revenue to distributors who actually contribute to the scale of usage based on the transaction volume and holdings of participating companies. In other words, the core change lies in revenue attribution: from centralized acquisition by the issuer to distribution based on distribution contribution. In the traditional stablecoin model, the issuer's profit logic is closer to a bank's net interest margin: users and payment platforms provide the circulation volume (similar to interest-free deposits), the issuer is responsible for allocating the underlying reserve assets, and obtains the main interest income. OUSD attempts to adjust this structure. By incorporating distribution partners into the revenue sharing system, the reserve returns, originally concentrated in the issuer's hands, are now flowing back to channels that generate actual usage, based on transaction volume and holding balances. For platforms like Stripe, Shopify, and Visa, this means that choosing a stablecoin is no longer just a matter of technology or clearing efficiency, but also begins to impact their revenue structure. By channeling their transaction volume and merchant funds into OUSD, they have the opportunity to participate in the interest rate spread that was previously exclusively enjoyed by the issuer. This default mechanism of shared reserve returns adds an economic layer to the evaluation of whether traditional enterprises should adopt stablecoins. In contrast, in the existing ecosystem dominated by Circle and Tether, issuers typically retain all reserve interest. Regardless of the transaction volume contributed by Stripe, Visa, or other merchants, most remain at the usage level and have difficulty participating in revenue sharing. Previously, platforms chose USDC primarily based on its instrumental attributes such as liquidity, settlement efficiency, and ecosystem maturity. OUSD aims to change this revenue structure. Within this framework, distribution channels will have clearer economic incentives. By directing transaction traffic to OUSD, distributors can share in the returns of reserve assets, making stablecoins not only payment channels but also financial instruments with profit-sharing attributes. Platforms that control merchant networks and transaction entry points thus gain the opportunity to participate in the underlying interest distribution. It is this incentive mechanism that makes distributors more proactive in driving adoption. Traditional stablecoins often rely on expanding their merchant base and channels one by one, while OUSD integrates multi-party distribution capabilities from the outset through an alliance model. This means that the distribution network itself becomes one of its key advantages, even though some mechanisms are still under development. Over 140 initial partners provide a distribution foundation covering both crypto and traditional finance, and the combination of different members corresponds to a large number of potential B2B payment scenarios. At the same time, the alliance includes both large payment companies and mainstream crypto exchanges, giving it strong deposit and withdrawal capabilities from the outset, which is one of the key conditions for stablecoins to enter the actual payment system. Essentially, this is more like a profit-sharing stablecoin structure designed for B2B distribution networks. The stablecoin market has generally followed two paths: centralized issuance and concentrated returns, represented by USDT and USDC, and decentralized solutions that emphasize decentralized governance but are weaker in distribution. OUSD attempts to find a balance between the two: maintaining the efficiency of centralized clearing while opening up the distribution network to profit sharing. While OUSD is nominally a decentralized stablecoin issuance, its profit structure resembles a collective negotiation initiated by a channel alliance regarding the issuer's interest rate spread. Therefore, it is understandable that the three largest stablecoin issuers—Circle, Tether, and PayPal—did not participate. For the issuers who are bypassed, the pressure from this model has already been reflected in the capital markets. Circle (NYSE: CRCL), a representative of US stock stablecoins, saw its stock price plummet by 17.5% that day, partly due to passive selling caused by the FTSE Russell semi-annual index restructuring. Circle was removed from several growth stock indices during the same period, triggering mechanical selling by tracking funds. However, even excluding this factor, the market's reaction to the OUSD news itself still points to a reassessment of the sustainability of the old model where issuers concentrate their profits. For Circle, how much liquidity OUSD can steal in the short term may not be the most important thing. After all, what's hardest for stablecoins to replace is the inertia built up by liquidity, trust, and compliance. However, the real issue of repricing reserve yield distribution is what's causing the most anxiety in the market and is also the most vulnerable part of Circle's business model. This vulnerability is primarily reflected in its revenue structure. Circle's revenue is highly dependent on interest rates. In 2025, its total revenue was $2.75 billion, the vast majority of which came from interest income on USDC reserve assets. Reserve income in the fourth quarter alone reached $733 million, accounting for over 95% of total revenue. The dollars deposited by users into USDC are used to buy short-term US Treasury bonds, and Circle earns its profits from the interest on these bonds. Circle also needs to pay a significant percentage of revenue to its core distributors. According to Circle's IPO prospectus and financial reports, Coinbase takes all the reserve income generated from USDC held on the Coinbase platform, while reserve income generated from other channels is split 50/50 between the two companies. In 2024, Circle paid Coinbase $908 million in distribution fees, accounting for approximately 54% of its total revenue. This means that for every $1 Circle earned, more than half was paid to Coinbase as distribution fees; and Coinbase itself neither issues USDC nor assumes reserve management responsibility. This revenue-sharing arrangement is difficult for Circle to unilaterally adjust. According to disclosures, this distribution agreement cannot be unilaterally terminated by Circle and automatically renews every three years, meaning it doesn't have complete control over pricing and negotiation. Whether future profit margins can improve largely depends on Circle's ability to establish more diversified distribution channels outside of Coinbase and reduce its dependence on a single core partner. The emergence of OUSD essentially aims to redistribute stablecoin reserve returns from issuers to distribution networks that control transaction volume and funding channels. This list of top partners, coupled with Stripe's extensive financial product line, gives this challenge real weight. Circle faces not only the challenge of maintaining USDC's size, but also the challenge of retaining control over reserve return distribution. It will be worth watching whether Circle adjusts its distribution strategy and partnership structure, and how other issuers and distributors who have not yet clearly aligned themselves will respond to this change. Conclusion: Challenges to the Implementation of OUSD OUSD's distribution network and deposit/withdrawal capabilities are its biggest potential advantages and a key link for stablecoins to transition from crypto-native tools to mainstream payment systems. However, whether this advantage can be realized depends on whether these channels can effectively migrate real business traffic such as default settlement paths, merchant acquiring, and cross-border fund pools. Meanwhile, reserve asset allocation, cross-regional liquidity arrangements, and multi-jurisdictional compliance frameworks still lack clear answers. More subtly, the internal interests within the alliance are also a factor. Coinbase remains deeply involved in and benefits from the USDC ecosystem, yet it joined OUSD as a founding member at this time, which is perhaps more noteworthy than the list of 140 companies itself. Coinbase and Circle's USDC revenue-sharing agreement has its first three-year renewal window expire in August 2026, less than two months after the OUSD alliance was announced. This timing makes OUSD not just a new stablecoin option, but also seems like an additional bargaining chip for Coinbase before renewal negotiations. Large channel providers clearly don't intend to bet on just one stablecoin; they value maintaining bargaining power among different issuers and stablecoin networks. Whether OUSD can ultimately achieve economies of scale depends on whether participants substantially drive traffic or continue to bet on multiple fronts. This is still uncertain. But regardless of the outcome, this alliance has broken an important assumption: that reserve returns are exclusively enjoyed by the issuer is not a natural rule of the stablecoin business model. In this sense, Open Standard is more like a business-level attempt at decentralization, pushing the profit margins that were originally concentrated in the hands of issuers like Circle and Tether to the platform that truly brings in trading volume and balance accumulation. The center hasn't disappeared; it's just that the issuer is no longer the sole and obvious recipient of the profits.

data.title: CZ: Competitor attacks are "too naive"; Greece's visa refusal is a lose-lose situation.
data.description:
Source: The Starting Block: CZ Interview

Translator: CryptoLeo; Editor: Qin Xiaofeng

Ten years is too long, seize the day. I hope people remember me as a "Crypto Guy."

Editor's Note: Binance founder Changpeng Zhao (CZ) recently gave an interview to The Block blog in Dubai, where he responded candidly to sensitive topics such as the inside story of Musk's $500 million investment, the Greek regulatory turmoil, and his experience surviving in prison.

He frankly admitted to a past where he "would rather ask for forgiveness than permission," reflecting that if he could do it again, he would "directly block all American users"; he asserted that crypto is the future of currency, predicting that Bitcoin may reach one million dollars by 2033; he revealed that his most frequently used AI is the free version of Kimi, simply because he "ran out of his paid quota." From a workaholic who worked 21 hours a day to a "seize the day" industry promoter, CZ's transformation and perseverance are all revealed in this exclusive interview.

I. Ten years is too long, seize the day, crypto is still the core and the future

Q: Let's talk about you and Musk first. You've never met him, but you invested $500 million when he acquired X. Why spend so much money?

Q: Let's talk about you and Musk first. You've never met him, but you invested $500 million when he acquired X.

CZ: To achieve financial freedom, you first need freedom of speech, and X is a very important platform for freedom of speech. While $500 million is a lot, it's only about 1% of Musk's $44 billion acquisition price. I'm glad that cryptocurrency companies can contribute to freedom of speech. This investment went through some twists and turns. I originally thought the deal would be completed in May 2022, but it was ultimately completed in October. At that time, many people said X wasn't worth $44 billion. I felt we should support capable entrepreneurs, and the result was quite good. Q: Why haven't you met with Musk? Were there opportunities but no success? CZ: We didn't deliberately avoid each other; our schedules are both quite busy. Even regarding that $500 million investment, I only exchanged about a dozen messages with him. On average, we probably only exchange a few messages a year. We're both very busy, though I'm not as busy now. I think our idea is: we only meet when necessary. Q: I think X's development over the past year has been very interesting. One trend is X's native payment system. What do you think the future of social payments will be like? CZ: Most payment solutions are limited to specific countries, such as Stripe and PayPal in the US; and WeChat and Alipay in China, all of which are country-specific. I hope X can overcome this. Cryptocurrency may be the first payment technology to break down national borders. I did speak with Musk when I learned that X announced X money. I sent Musk a message on X: Can we become partners? He said X money handles all fiat currencies, so they won't touch cryptocurrencies, but I do hope X can become a global platform—currency should be global, and we have no reason to divide currencies by country. For example, you can have your own monetary policy, but you're using a global currency. I really hope X can do that. And crypto won't take away a piece of the existing payment model's "cake"—we all hope for better services that allow the industry to grow larger. Humans should be able to transact and do business globally. Q: Speaking of you and Musk, Musk is probably the most influential person in the world. Would you like to become the most influential person in the crypto space? What do you hope people will remember you for in the future? Or something else? CZ: Wealth rankings aren't important. I don't want to compete with Musk or anyone else, and I don't really care how I'll be remembered. Regarding this question, I hope that when I look back on my life before I die, I can say to myself, "I've done my best." I always say that, which also means that if I could, I should have done more. So I'm currently in a phase where I'm helping other founders; not leading, but being a enabler. I think Musk is amazing; he's driven so many incredibly difficult companies and projects, and he deserves to be the richest person in the world. I'm different from him in this respect. Binance is a lucky and successful platform; we've been able to help over 300 million users globally, and many users write me thank-you notes—that's a great achievement. In the next phase, I just want to help other founders. We'll see what the future holds. Q: Besides Giggle Academy and Yzi Labs, do you have any bigger plans? What are your main focuses right now? CZ: I actually divide my time into four core parts: Giggle Academy; Yzi Labs investments; advising other governments on cryptocurrency regulation (which probably takes up most of my time); and helping other founders in the crypto ecosystem. These things take up my time, and I don't make any big plans; things move too fast. Five or ten years is too long; I only focus on the present. I only focus on what's important in the next few months, what I should do, and then allocate my time accordingly, adjusting myself every few months. I think people's mindsets change over time. I worked really hard running Binance for six or seven years, almost 20 to 21 hours a day, taking 20 to 30 calls and attending various meetings. It was a very meaningful job, but also very tiring, requiring good health. Now, however, I'm helping other founders and focusing on broader things, learning about biotechnology, AI, and other areas. Cryptocurrency is at my core; currency won't disappear, and neither will crypto technology. While many have recently turned to AI, cryptocurrency will always exist. AI needs funding, and they will use cryptocurrency.

II. Preferring to Ask for Forgiveness Rather Than Ask for Permission

Q: You just released your new book, which is full of anecdotes and perhaps some little-known stories. But I'd like to delve deeper into the founding of Binance and its origins. A small group of you built a very high-performance platform, using BNB as an incentive to encourage people to trade. In the year I first interviewed you, Binance's trading volume surpassed all other exchanges. You probably didn't expect it to grow so rapidly. What's the secret to your success?

CZ: First, you have to have a solid foundation, a strong team, and a truly good product. We are fortunate that Binance occupies a niche market that others haven't, such as during the ICO boom of 2017 when growth was very rapid. Later, the market changed—DeFi became popular, NFT became popular, etc., but we have always maintained our leading position. A fundamental principle is how we protect our users. Even in the early days of being unlicensed and unregulated, when operating an exchange platform, users need to know if you are protecting them. For a crypto exchange, every policy and every decision you make will affect people's funds, regardless of whether there is regulation or a license. I think this is the key reason why many people choose Binance. I could give countless examples of how Binance made decisions that, in the short term, harmed its own finances but protected its users. Users reward Binance by staying, trading happily, and paying small fees—fees that, over time, generate far greater revenue than a platform could earn from sporadic transactions. When China forced some projects to exit the market, we had to return fiat currency to users. Binance used $6 million of its $15 million in reserves to return to users, a one-time investment of 40%. These examples of protecting users are also mentioned in my book, though many details are omitted. Users will check our reserve accounts to understand our decisions; they know we always put users first. In the long run, this is the key to Binance's success. Many other platforms can imitate your product, technology, and other things, but when it comes to financial decisions, they are always short-sighted. Q: You've been under strict regulatory scrutiny for years. Regarding Binance's penalties in the US and your imprisonment, from an outsider's perspective, what were the reasons for your quick decision to pay the fine and go to the US? Have you ever regretted it? Is there anything you want to change? CZ: Looking back, there were some things I could have changed, but information was limited at the time. "To succeed, you have to take risks. I'd rather ask for forgiveness than permission," is a quote I learned from Richard Branson, who wrote about six or seven books, all of which touched on the topic of Lost Virginity. (Odaily Note: Lost virginity encourages people to take risks and challenge tradition; each new adventure is a step into the unknown, like losing virginity, full of tension, excitement, risk, and uncertainty.) The US government accuses me of not following the rules, but I am a rule-abiding person in life. My way of thinking often deviates from the norm, but I have never truly broken the rules. Cryptocurrency is an emerging industry, and everything I've done is simply more about following the technology route. Looking back, what I want to change is that if I were building a platform now, I would directly block all US users without permission, and with permission, I would go through the formal channels. I think this is probably one obvious area where I need to change. Besides that, there are many other mistakes, most of which are self-correcting. Flying to the US to deal with the charges was just to put an end to it. It was really painful; I never expected to go to jail for it. No one goes to jail for violating bank secrecy rules once; the worst-case scenario is a few months of house arrest, meaning I can still access the internet since I don't go out. But the outcome was more serious than I anticipated, but I pulled through. Q: Most people feel very scared and uneasy about going to jail. What was your experience like in prison? CZ: At the time, I just felt a lot of uncertainty, that unknown anticipation of what the future might hold. Like I said before, no one can tell you exactly what prison is like before you go there. There are many different prisons in the United States alone. It's estimated that there are 53 different prison systems, each potentially different in each state, and each system has different levels. Each city, each prison, holds two or three thousand people, like a small town. They have their own rules, and no one can tell you exactly what it's like inside. This uncertainty is always very worrying. Moreover, Bloomberg, the Wall Street Journal, and other media outlets have reported that I will become the richest person ever imprisoned in the United States. My lawyer told me I was likely to become a primary target for extortion. I went to prison with that expectation. Once you're in prison, you start wondering when you'll get out? Will they give me more trouble? Will there be other things that will keep you there? The uncertainty is really, really worrying. Q: I remember hearing some anecdotes from you, like prison guards asking you if they should buy Bitcoin, and your lawyers warning you to be careful in prison. Have you ever encountered extortion? CZ: Actually, I've never been extorted. I joined a group soon after entering prison. My prison advisor (formerly the warden) told me: "CZ, don't go, don't join any groups, just stay silent. That's the best way to avoid trouble." But another prison advisor who had also served time told me I needed to join a small group for protection as soon as possible; being alone would definitely lead to bullying. Once you're in prison, you have no choice but to join a group. I joined a group called the Pacific Islanders, which was only half Asian; there was also a larger group called the Mexicans, which were mostly drug lords and Mexicans. Once you join a group, it does offer some protection, so people don't cause trouble. If a dispute arises, they negotiate with a group representative, so I wasn't extorted. But I do get asked, "Hey, I heard you're rich, how much money do you have?" I just laugh it off and say, "I don't know what you're talking about, what money?" Q: You mentioned a few weeks ago that you donated $2 million to Prison Professor. Was this donation because of your prison experience, where you learned that most of them really need reintegration into society, and you wanted to help them because of your own experience? CZ: Yes, more specifically, Prison Professor was founded by Michael Santos, one of my prison consultants. He was sentenced to 45 years in prison for first-time drug trafficking, and even murder carries a maximum sentence of 10 years. He served only 26 years due to good behavior, and after his release, he was very sympathetic to my case and said he would help me. Michael founded and runs Prison Professor, helping inmates reintegrate into society before their release through education. He wrote six books and earned bachelor's and master's degrees while incarcerated. He got married with 10 years left on his sentence; prisons mostly deal with divorces, and they don't know how to handle marriage. Michael gave me a lot of advice on how to survive in prison. So after I got out, I looked at his project, which was great, and I gave him $500,000 every six months or so. Four transfers have been made, totaling two million. We kept this a secret until two weeks ago when he said he wanted to go public to be more inspiring for other inmates. The actual person in charge of this project is a former incarcerated individual and a cryptocurrency practitioner. He also applied to me to introduce cryptocurrency education into prisons, and I agreed. Later, he copied and pasted a lot of Binance Academy content into his educational materials, which I thought was great because you can't learn about AI, blockchain, and cryptocurrency in prison, and you don't even have access to the internet. Many people don't even know how to use a smartphone after their release. Many people who have spent 10 or 20 years in prison can't find jobs after they get out, so what are they supposed to do? They'll re-offend, creating a vicious cycle. I've been in prison, and I think a large portion of people shouldn't be there at all; the law and the justice system aren't perfect. When Michael was released, drugs were legal in most states, so the crimes he committed were now completely legal. For other people who come to me asking for money, I think, I don't know if this person is a scammer. But I think Michael is a good person, so I support his cause, which makes me feel comfortable. He also has some financial means and donates his money to charities or Prison Professors. I think it's a very good thing to be able to help him. Q: You also mentioned OKCoin in your book, and your experience working at that exchange. I know you don't want to talk about this in detail, but OKX founder Star has been publicly attacking Binance and you personally. I just want to understand the story behind this from your perspective, your experience at OKCoin, how you parted ways, and if you parted amicably, how do you view the situation now? CZ: He Yi and I worked at OKCoin in 2014. I left in early 2015, and I thought we parted on a friendly note. After I left, Star and Roger Ver had a dispute over the bitcoin.com domain, and I got involved. At the time, I issued a disclaimer not to get involved. A few years later, He Yi and I, along with several co-founders, founded Binance, and we became very successful. Since then, we've focused on the business. For others, this might seem like a personal psychological imbalance. I always advise teams to focus on their business. We build the best products, provide the best user protection, develop the best compliance programs, and work with regulators around the world to advance the development of cryptocurrency. That's what we do. There are many people who are jealous of our success. It's okay to be jealous of me, but attacking competitors is childish, so I usually don't respond much. Regarding my private life, I've been officially divorced for many years. These kinds of things are irrelevant to users and business and shouldn't be a focus. The industry will only improve if we focus on providing the best products for users. I'm more concerned about other competitors who are focused on their own products now. III. Europe needs crypto; rejecting Binance's license application is a lose-lose situation. Q: For years, many people in the industry have accused Binance of various regulatory and compliance deficiencies. A few weeks ago, an anonymous source told me that ECB President Christine Lagarde was involved in Greece's decision to block Binance's MiCA application, which Greece had intended to approve. Binance has now made it clear that it is withdrawing its application in Greece and will try to apply through another member state. Does facing regulatory hurdles in another important jurisdiction give you a bit of PTSD? CZ: Yes, I received the same information as you. There is no direct information indicating that Lagarde blocked anything. I did ask the Binance team about the MiCA process, and what I understand is that at least one or two regulators told us they were about to approve it, but other policy factors were also at play. In fact, two countries wanted to grant Binance a MiCA license; they wanted Binance to be present and to boost the economy, but there were indeed some other factors opposing Binance's license, and ultimately Binance reviewed and withdrew its MiCA application. As a stakeholder, I hope Binance can get along well with regulators and obtain a license, as this will not be a smooth process. Binance has faced challenges in many countries in the past, such as Japan, where Japan issued a warning to Binance as early as 2018. However, in 2022, Binance received permission from the Japanese Financial Services Agency to return to Japan, and we were actually indirectly invited back. By early 2023, Binance had obtained a full Japanese license. Binance Japan is now fully operational, and SoftBank Japan is one of its major shareholders. Furthermore, we also experienced a difficult period in Singapore. We had to withdraw from Singapore at the end of 2021. After Binance withdrew from Singapore, most users switched to FTX, which is invested in by Singapore's sovereign wealth fund, Temasek. Binance initially faced challenges in many other countries as well, but over time we were able to overcome them. Challenges always arise, and political factors also play a role. Overall, Greece's current rejection of Binance is a loss for Binance and a loss for Europe. It's a lose-lose situation. I hope this can be resolved as soon as possible. Q: Currently, the two largest groups in the cryptocurrency space, Tether and Binance, are unable to operate in Europe. What are your thoughts on this? CZ: Generally speaking, Europe is relatively conservative. Europe has 27 national governments and the European Union; their political structure is more complex. Even at the beginning of X's launch, tens of thousands of people were prosecuted or arrested for posting content on X. Every government has different policies, but that's okay. I think we'll see different countries implementing different regulatory policies. Those countries with truly forward-thinking cryptocurrency regulations will benefit from cryptocurrencies, while those that don't will lag behind a bit. Over time, they'll correct themselves, so I'm not too worried. Q: My last question focuses on Binance, especially Binance US. Can you explain the difference between Binance and Binance US? Also, given the many changes in the regulatory environment, what are Binance's overall plans for the future of the US market? CZ: These are two separate platforms. Binance US was established in 2019 and is a completely independent entity. Binance US has its own trading platform and wallet, all of which are separate from binance.com. Binance US has always held a license in the United States. Throughout my entire dispute with the US government, Binance US was never involved because they consistently adhered to the rules. Binance US did indeed face a civil lawsuit from the SEC, losing its banking services for several years and a significant amount of business during that period, but it remained profitable. Eventually, Binance US restored its banking channels and now offers zero-fee transactions to some major payment providers. Fourth, we should be wary of AI; even someone as powerful as CZ uses free AI software. Q: You've talked a lot about AI, such as AI agents typically using encryption technology. What AI applications do you use, ChatGPT or Claude? CZ: I've used many AI tools, but the one I use most often is probably Kimi. It's simpler, cheaper, faster, and usable everywhere. I set up my own OpenClaw and tried to programmatically clean up my Gmail inbox. I always receive a lot of spam, but I couldn't program it to delete most of it because many of the spam emails were from people I didn't know, requesting meetings. The AI ​​assistant always thought all these requests were important, and I kept thinking, "I don't know those people," and after a while, I gave up. When Claude coding was popular, I paid a lot of money for it, but my subscription expired. Now I use the free version, which has a usage limit; for example, when I reach the 25-use limit, I switch to another one. This has also exposed me to various AI agents, which has actually benefited me. Q: When you were creating Binance, if you had powerful models like Claude and ChatGPT, do you think you could have created Binance much faster? CZ: Definitely faster, probably 20% to 50% faster. However, we still need to be careful when using AI. I think we can let AI write code, helping programmers write code, while the programmers must review every line of code and understand its meaning, otherwise you can't maintain it. Another very useful feature is that AI is excellent at finding vulnerabilities. For example, AI gave the Binance team a version that only allowed viewing of binance.com, allowing them to discover platform vulnerabilities earlier than others. This actually improves the security of the industry. Q: There have been many DeFi hacks this year, and many experts I've spoken to have stated that this is because people are using AI tools to find vulnerabilities; these people are constantly attacking and testing systems. Are you worried that, due to the power of these tools, the global financial system could suffer extremely serious attacks? How can we deal with this situation? CZ: There are several ways to prevent this. One is to use these tools yourself first. Another is to set up multiple security safeguards and not put all your eggs in one basket. AI just makes vulnerabilities discovered faster. For example, you need to ensure that a hacking attack will not affect all your wallet funds. This is why we need hot wallets, multi-signature, and various technologies for security isolation, as well as code wallets. Even code wallets may not be completely immune to quantum attacks; you must upgrade your code wallet technology. Nothing is permanently 100% secure; you must keep up with technological advancements, which is an advantage of large platforms. Large platforms have the resources to continuously improve security capabilities, while small platforms, due to their scale limitations, may not have enough manpower and resources to support security. Therefore, it makes sense for large platforms to invest billions of dollars in security alone, which is what Binance has done. Many DeFi vulnerabilities are actually not large in monetary terms, such as millions or tens of millions. But such attacks are actually beneficial to the industry because once these vulnerabilities occur, we will know how to deal with and mitigate their impact. V. Not fully understanding STRC, but the market overreacted to Strategy's coin sale. Q: In the past two years, Michael Saylor has become one of the most influential figures in the Bitcoin space, especially last year when Saylor developed these different Bitcoin-based products. Now, the STRC trading price has fallen from 100 to around 70 dollars. Are you worried about this? What are your thoughts on Saylor and Strategy? CZ: To be honest, I've tried many times to understand STRC, but I don't think I fully understand it. I'm not really qualified to comment on Strategy, but I think these financial products are often too complex, too elaborately designed, and they often rely on multiple layers of leverage. When these kinds of specialized products become too complex, parts of them become black boxes. I think Saylor is very smart; he understands how publicly traded financial companies operate. But the market's view of him is either godlike or demonic; he's probably somewhere in between, perhaps closer to godlike. And when Strategy sold 32 bitcoins, the market overreacted a bit; STRC has to pay dividends and has to sell some bitcoins at some point. They held nearly a million Bitcoins and only sold 32. The market blames him for triggering the BTC drop, saying people are being too harsh on him and we need to be more lenient. But from a derivatives perspective, I don't fully understand how it's leveraged, so I can't comment too much on that. Q: Yes, he used Bitcoin as underlying collateral to build very complex financial instruments. One argument is that Bitcoin is extremely valuable, great for businesses to hold as an asset, and that digital credit can be built on top of it. I'd like to hear your thoughts on this argument? CZ: There's an implicit assumption that Bitcoin's value will continue to rise over time. Therefore, using Bitcoin as the underlying asset to build leveraged products will largely depend on Bitcoin's price fluctuations. Since Bitcoin is highly volatile, it may not be the best choice as the underlying asset for leveraged products. This is based on my limited understanding; I don't know all the details of its complex design. Saylor once tried to explain it to me for 15 minutes, and although I nodded frequently, I didn't fully understand (at the time, I thought I understood, but looking back, some parts are still unclear). Overall, I don't think Saylor is the kind of person who would create fake products; he has credibility and is a staunch Bitcoin supporter. We all believe that Bitcoin will perform well in the long run. It's just that the current Bitcoin price drop is making everyone unhappy, and they'll find various ways to vent their emotions. This kind of emotional outburst has occurred at different times, and I've had similar outbursts myself. Q: I'd like to continue discussing DAT. I've spoken with the CEA Industries team over the past year or two about the value proposition of BNB-based DAT. But the dispute between CEA and Binance has been making headlines. I wonder if you'd be willing to talk about it, and how things are progressing. Could you share your overall perspective? CZ: Of course, I don't know much about CEA, including all the details of the dispute. Binance provided CEA with approximately $100 million worth of BNB, roughly 20% of their total funding. Binance did vet the team to get them up and running. I think this is good for them and helps them attract other investors to buy shares in CEA. But a lot did happen during that process. Recommended reading: "CZ Also Scammed? Bloodbath for Control of BNB's Treasury, CEA Industries" (https://www.odaily.news/zh-CN/post/5209913) Now, some members of the Yzi Labs team are going to manage CEA. My initial thought was that Yzi Labs could continue as a minority shareholder and wouldn't have to deal with the affairs of a listed company, etc. But later, CEA's stock price fell below one dollar, basically meaning the stock price was lower than the value of their BNB holdings. This wasn't good for investors, and several people from the Yzi Labs team volunteered to step forward to try to resolve the issue. I believe they already have a solution, and I haven't done much. Several members of Yzi Labs also co-invested in CEA, using their own money. This is one of the reasons I'm not too worried about it; they have a vested interest in this matter. Q: Do you think Aster can catch up with Hyperliquid? CZ: I'm just an advisor to Aster, mainly responsible for product and technical guidance. Hyperliquid is doing very well, but no product can stay on the top spot forever. If Aster performs well in the future, it still has a chance.

VI. Not a Bitcoin maximalist, but Bitcoin could potentially reach $1 million in 2 cycles

Q: I want to talk to you about Bitcoin one last time, because I am a fervent supporter and advocate of Bitcoin. Your story about getting into Bitcoin is interesting, selling your house to invest in Bitcoin. What do you think of Bitcoin's current state? What will its future trend be?

CZ:I am still very bullish on Bitcoin. So far, Bitcoin has followed the four-year cycle very closely. We are in a crypto winter. Bitcoin has retraced about 50% from its all-time high, but this is normal and not a big problem.

In the long run, AI needs funding and encryption technology. We need a better monetary system, and our monetary system won't disappear because of AI. Blockchain will continue to exist; it's not just about money. Anything that requires verifiable proof can be done on the blockchain—land ownership, citizenship, etc. AI will accelerate the development of blockchain. Q: Why aren't you a Bitcoin maximalist and only invest in Bitcoin? CZ: I am a staunch supporter of Bitcoin, but I believe that if there were only Bitcoin, the industry would actually develop much slower. The Bitcoin network is already saturated; we went through the block size debate before Ethereum, BNB Chain, and other blockchains were able to rise. If we only had the Bitcoin blockchain, we would all try to innovate on top of it, which would be very limiting—upgrading the Bitcoin protocol requires a considerable amount of work, and the Bitcoin protocol is quite limited in terms of programmability. That's why we have so many other blockchains that are innovating, which actually drives the entire industry to develop faster. Bitcoin can see innovation, such as ordinals. Having millions of blockchains may not be necessary, but having 10, 20, 30, or even hundreds of innovating blockchains can be a good thing. Hundreds might already be considered long-tail, because many blockchains won't make a difference, but occasionally someone will have a new idea, and it's difficult to implement these ideas on existing blockchains. That's why we support diversity. Q: You often have great insights into the price and value of Bitcoin. Do you often think about the valuation of Bitcoin? Do you think its dollar price is that important? CZ: I don't think about that. I think it's very difficult to predict a specific price at a specific time. One view is that as the total market capitalization increases, volatility decreases. Each rise may be slower and smaller than the previous one. The opposing view is that Bitcoin is far from saturated. In terms of personal wealth, holding Bitcoin may account for less than 1% of total wealth, not yet reaching saturation. Therefore, the overall demand for Bitcoin or cryptocurrencies may be very large. I think the last cycle was a bit weak. The price of Bitcoin doubled in the last cycle; hopefully, the next cycle will improve. If the next cycle grows fivefold, the price will exceed $500,000 or $600,000; then in the next cycle after that, the cycle in 2033, it would only need to double to reach $1 million, which is entirely possible. Q: The last question is, what do you hope people will remember you for in the future? CZ: There's a very simple answer. President Trump called us "Crypto Guys," and I also hope people remember me as a Crypto Guy.

data.title: The launch of new stablecoins by 140 major players may still have a limited impact on the market landscape.
data.description:
Author: Gu Yu, ChainCatcher

On the first day of the second half of 2026, the stablecoin market saw a rare variable: stablecoin company Open Standard announced the launch of Open USD (OUSD), which has gained support from over 140 companies, including traditional financial, payment, technology, and crypto giants such as Visa, Stripe, Mastercard, BlackRock, Standard Chartered Bank, Google, and Coinbase.

Over the past decade, countless stablecoins have challenged USDT and USDC, but most have fallen victim to two realities: USDT monopolizes exchanges and offshore liquidity, while USDC dominates US compliance and institutional access.

Whether it's GUSD, FDUSD, TUSD, or various algorithmic stablecoins and regional stablecoins, they will ultimately find it difficult to shake the core network effect of USDT and USDC. The difference with OUSD is that it's not driven by a single exchange, payment company, or issuer, but rather by a group of fiercely competing traditional giants. The signal it sends is clear: as stablecoin penetration increases, the traditional financial system is unwilling to be constrained by native crypto stablecoin systems any longer. As a significant milestone in the stablecoin landscape, OUSD is likely to change the existing stablecoin landscape and significantly increase the adoption rate of stablecoins in the global financial system. I. How does OUSD differ from other stablecoins? The most distinctive feature of OUSD is its consortium nature. According to the design of Open Standard, it employs collaborative management, possessing an independent management team and governance mechanism responsible for overseeing the design and operation of Open USD. Unlike stablecoins issued by a single company, Open Standard attempts to allow participants to make decisions based on shared interests rather than the interests of a single entity. This makes it resemble a stablecoin version of an "industry standards organization." The core of the traditional stablecoin revenue model is that the issuer pockets the returns from reserve assets, while both minting and redemption incur transaction fees. The business models of Tether and Circle heavily rely on interest income generated from reserves. Especially during periods of high interest rates, stablecoin issuers can reap substantial profits simply by increasing circulation. OUSD attempts to change this distribution mechanism. Open Standard states that OUSD will return the majority of the profits generated by its reserves, after deducting a small management fee, to participants who adopt and distribute the currency; minting and redemption will be free of charge. This is precisely OUSD's key difference: it doesn't simply challenge USDC with "more compliance" or "more transparency," but rather challenges the existing stablecoin business model with its profit distribution. For Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, Coinbase, exchanges, and payment companies, the most difficult aspect of stablecoins isn't understanding the technology, but rather why they should distribute currency for others while handing over reserve profits to the issuers. The design of OUSD essentially redistributes stablecoin revenue from issuers to distribution channels. If USDC was a stablecoin driven by the early collaboration between Circle and Coinbase, then OUSD attempts to extend this channel-sharing mechanism to the entire payments and finance industry. II. The Implications Behind the Team and Partner List According to Open Standard's official website and LinkedIn data, the company's two currently disclosed members both come from Bridge, a stablecoin payment platform acquired by Stripe for $1.1 billion. The CEO is Bridge co-founder and CEO Zach Abrams, and the other founding member, Ben O'Neill, whose specific position is not specified, is Bridge's Head of Liquidity. Meanwhile, both individuals' LinkedIn profiles show Bridge as currently employed, indicating they are working at Open Standard under the arrangement of its parent company, Stripe, reflecting Stripe's particularly prominent influence within Open Standard. In fact, Stripe is also one of the most proactive companies among the 140+ companies, having already posted on X announcing that it is making OpenUSD the default option for businesses using stablecoins on Stripe. This is not surprising. Stripe is one of the most aggressive traditional payment companies in its stablecoin strategy over the past two years. From acquiring Bridge to acquiring wallet infrastructure Privy, and then participating in OUSD, Stripe's path has become increasingly clear: it is not content with simply being an integrator of stablecoin payments, but aims to control the key nodes of stablecoin issuance, wallets, compliance, and distribution. It's worth noting that companies like MoneyGram and Western Union, which had previously announced their own stablecoin plans, are still present in the OUSD-related cooperation landscape; however, PayPal, which has already issued PYUSD, is not on the list. This indicates a divergence in the attitudes of traditional payment giants towards stablecoins: some choose to build their own, some choose alliances, and some may be forced to reassess their siloed strategies. Even more noteworthy is the list of exchanges. OUSD's crypto partners include Coinbase, Bybit, OKX, Crypto.com, Gemini, Bitso, and Upbit's parent company, Dunamu, but Binance is conspicuously absent from the top-tier exchanges. This absence is not accidental. Binance has previously promoted stablecoins like BUSD, FDUSD, and TUSD, all driven by its own ecosystem, but their adoption rates have been disappointing. For Binance, a new stablecoin jointly promoted by Coinbase, Stripe, Visa, and others may not necessarily align with its strategic interests. The presence of Coinbase is even more nuanced. As a core partner of Circle's USDC stablecoin, Coinbase has been a crucial promoter of USDC's development. The two companies have a deep-rooted relationship, sharing not only equity investment but also USDC revenue-sharing agreements. Coinbase's participation in the OUSD ecosystem reflects its current tendency to diversify its investments. As USDC's advantages in compliance and distribution channels weakened, Circle's negative impact was immediately reflected in the capital market, with its stock price falling over 17% to $62 that evening, nearing its IPO price and down over 55% from its previous high of $140.

III. Crypto Industry Experts Generally Believe the Impact is Limited

Despite OUSD's large partner lineup and the significant impact on Circle's stock price, analyses from numerous crypto industry opinion leaders on X generally suggest that while OUSD has clear application scenarios, due to issues such as liquidity and execution, it is unlikely to have a substantial impact on USDT and USDC, and may even be beneficial to them.

Lorenzo Valente, Director of Digital Asset Research at ARK Invest, a firm under Cathie Wood, raised questions about the organizational structure. In his view, "everyone owns" almost always means no one is responsible.

"I don't believe an organization that looks like a 500-company DAO can act quickly enough to be effective in the long run. Who decides the IPO strategy? Capital allocation? Or anything else?" Lorenzo Valente posted. "Open Standard is a competitor DAO that doesn't really commit to anything. I'd bet on two operators that can deliver products unilaterally, not a committee that needs to consult 500 competitors." ARK Invest's attitude is also reflected in its actions. Public data shows that Ark Invest bought another 52,455 shares of Circle ($3.28 million) last night. According to incomplete statistics, Ark Invest has purchased more than 4.5 million shares of Circle in the past six months, currently worth over $250 million. Crypto KOL Lufei holds a similar view. The competition among stablecoins isn't about who has the most impressive shareholder list, but rather who possesses liquidity, network effects, compliance capabilities, business development execution, and capital allocation abilities. OpenUSD's biggest problem is the abundance of large institutions, fragmented interests, slow governance, and weak economic incentives, making it difficult for it to execute quickly, take risks, and build a truly stablecoin network like Circle or Tether. Alliance co-founder Imran Khan also stated that alliance stablecoins will win in corporate fund management, cross-company settlements, and behind-the-scenes payment tracks, where end-users may not even know which stablecoin they are using. However, OUSD won't automatically steal market share from USDT or USDC. Circle/Tether's biggest moat isn't technology, but liquidity, integration, and global distribution. Blue Fox believes that OUSD may capture a significant share of the market in areas such as enterprise payments/remittances/merchants/RWA, driving overall growth in the stablecoin market. However, this is not the end for USDC/USDT; each will eventually find its own use case. For example, USDT still has opportunities in crypto trading/speculation/DeFi/emerging markets, USDC has opportunities in institutional/compliance/DeFi, while OUSD has greater potential in enterprise scenarios (where enterprises use stablecoins for large-scale payments, merchant settlements, cross-border transfers, etc.). The honest and straightforward Mr. Mai stated that once traditional financial institutions open payment channels for cryptocurrencies, users will ultimately have multiple stablecoins to choose from, with USDT, USDC, or OUD being the user's decision. This is similar to Stripe supporting multiple payment methods, or Binance offering its own Busd, USDT, and USDC, or HyperLiquid offering its own USDH and USDC. If these 3 billion users can join, it could easily cut off at least 80% of the market share from USDC and USDT. Colin Wu, founder of Wu Blockchain, believes that excessive compliance is a disadvantage for OUSD. In his view, stablecoins, like other products in the crypto world, satisfy the need to "evade regulation." As the scale expands, compliance will inevitably follow, making it impossible to evade regulation, thus reducing demand. The difference between stablecoins and traditional digital dollars is no longer significant. Therefore, stablecoins still rely heavily on cryptocurrency applications. Considering these points, OUSD is unlikely to immediately shake the market share of USDT and USDC. The true moat of stablecoins has never been technology and compliance, but rather liquidity, trading pairs, payment networks, and user habits—all of which require years or even longer to accumulate. Meanwhile, OUSD will significantly increase the adoption rate of stablecoins within the traditional financial system, and USDC will also benefit in this process. However, compared to challenging USDT and USDC in the short term, OUSD's greater significance lies in sending a signal to the market: traditional finance is no longer satisfied with simply "using stablecoins," but is beginning to actively participate in the construction of stablecoin infrastructure. As banks, payment institutions, internet platforms, and crypto companies work together to promote the adoption of stablecoins, the overall market's ceiling will rise accordingly.

data.title: Binance's suspension of EU services reflects the era of licensing in the crypto market.
data.description:
MiCA is not the end for the crypto industry, but rather the beginning of its financialization, licensing, and institutionalization. The Binance incident illustrates that the true moat of crypto platforms is no longer just technology, users, and liquidity, but regulatory trust, governance structure, and licensing capabilities.

On July 1, 2026, the EU MiCA transition period ends.

The most noteworthy aspect of this day is not that Europe has begun regulating crypto assets. In fact, whether it's mainland China, the US, Hong Kong, Singapore, or the EU, major jurisdictions have already intervened in digital asset regulation in different ways.

What is truly noteworthy is that the EU has chosen a very clear path—neither a simple ban nor continued laissez-faire, but rather, while acknowledging the existence of crypto asset services, it is reshaping market order with a unified licensing system.

Binance is currently the most representative case in this transformation.

Binance, Not Just a Simple "Withdrawal from Europe" Many reports summarize this event as "Binance leaving the EU." This statement is impactful, but not entirely accurate. A more accurate statement is: Binance did not abandon its strategic intention to enter the European market, but under MiCA's licensing rules, it temporarily lost the compliant channel to continue serving EU clients. These are not the same thing. The former resembles a voluntary withdrawal by the company, while the latter indicates that regulatory thresholds have truly taken effect. In the past, crypto platforms often viewed themselves as global internet platforms. An app, a website, and a wallet entry point were enough to serve users worldwide. However, after MiCA, EU regulators redefined this issue: If you provide trading, custody, exchange, consulting, portfolio management, or other services to EU clients, you are not an ordinary internet product, but rather providing crypto asset financial services. Since it's a financial service, it must be subject to financial regulation. This is the underlying logic of MiCA. It's not asking how strong your technology is, but whether you are qualified to operate in the European financial market. MiCA is not against crypto, but against "unregulated financialization." Many people easily interpret MiCA as Europe suppressing the crypto industry. I think this understanding is too simplistic. MiCA doesn't mean crypto assets can't exist, nor does it mean stablecoins, trading, custody, and exchange services are worthless. What it truly expresses is: these services can exist, but they must be within a regulatory framework. In other words, the EU isn't trying to eliminate crypto assets, but rather to financialize crypto asset service providers. In the past, the industry liked to say "code is law." But MiCA's answer is direct: code is not law; licenses are the entry ticket. Why? Because once a digital asset project starts involving customer assets, transaction matching, asset custody, profit arrangements, stablecoins, payment settlement, and secondary market liquidity, it's no longer just a technical issue. It becomes a financial issue. The core of financial issues is never about how beautifully the code is written, but about how responsibility is assigned. Where is the customer's money kept? What happens if the platform goes bankrupt? Who is responsible if assets are misappropriated? Who compensates for hacker attacks? Are there any conflicts of interest in listing coins? Is there any market manipulation in the trading? Is anti-money laundering implemented? Who will the regulators contact if problems arise? These questions cannot be answered by white papers, community slogans, or even by claiming "we are Web3 innovators." They can only be answered through systems, licenses, capital, internal controls, audits, compliance, and ongoing oversight. Therefore, MiCA is not truly cleaning up crypto technology, but rather the unregulated financialization disguised as technology. The real barrier has shifted from technology to licenses. In the past, the barriers to entry in the crypto industry were primarily technology and user traffic. Whoever had a more stable trading system, more cryptocurrencies, better liquidity, faster user growth, and better community building had a greater chance of succeeding. But after MiCA, the primary barrier has changed. Technology remains important, but it's no longer a prerequisite for entry. A license is the prerequisite. This is also the symbolic meaning of the Binance incident. Binance's technological capabilities, trading depth, and user base are not weak. However, regulatory scrutiny isn't about how many users you have, but whether you can be included in the European financial regulatory system. A license isn't about registering a company, submitting a set of documents, or making a compliance gesture. A license is essentially a form of regulatory trust. What regulators need to determine is: Is your platform worthy of being allowed to serve local investors? Is your company structure clear? Is your past compliance record reliable? Are your management team qualified? Is your customer asset protection adequate? If problems arise, can the responsibility be pinned on a specific entity? This is the logic of traditional financial regulation. The significance of MiCA lies in the fact that it formally brought this logic into the crypto industry. Binance being stuck shows that MiCA is not just for show. If it were just a small exchange that didn't obtain a license, the market might not be too shaken. But Binance is different. Binance is one of the world's largest cryptocurrency exchanges. Its sheer size makes its setbacks under MiCA all the more telling: Europe isn't just engaging in symbolic regulation; it's genuinely trying to reshape the market order. Several changes are likely to occur in the European cryptocurrency market after MiCA. First, smaller platforms will be forced out. Many small exchanges, wallets, and custody providers previously survived on a light-asset, low-cost, and cross-border business model. However, under the MiCA framework, compliance, auditing, internal controls, anti-money laundering, and customer asset protection will all become hard costs. Second, licensed institutions will erode market share. Authorized exchanges, custodians, payment institutions, and bank-affiliated digital asset service providers will gain stronger customer trust and institutional advantages. Third, traditional financial institutions may re-enter the market. In the past, banks were hesitant to engage with crypto assets due to unclear regulations. Now, with the regulations in place, traditional financial institutions are provided with a compliant path to enter the digital asset market. Fourth, regulatory arbitrage opportunities will shrink. Many platforms have historically sought regulatory loopholes across different jurisdictions. MiCA aims to unify the EU market and reduce such cross-border regulatory arbitrage. Therefore, the Binance incident is not an isolated event, but rather the beginning of a restructuring of the European crypto market. The future European crypto market is likely to no longer be "a single global exchange app serving everyone," but rather a regulated market comprised of "licensed exchanges, banks, custodians, payment institutions, and compliant technology companies." The most valuable lesson from MiCA is not just how Europe regulates crypto assets, but its reminder to all digital asset projects: Regulation will be present whenever financial attributes are involved. This judgment applies not only to mainland China, but also to the EU. In any jurisdiction, once a digital asset project begins to involve public financing, expected returns, asset custody, transaction matching, stablecoins, payment settlement, fund-like structures, securitization, and secondary market liquidity, regulation will inevitably follow. The difference lies in the regulatory approaches across different jurisdictions. The EU has opted for a MiCA-style licensing system. Hong Kong and Singapore have chosen regulated digital asset markets. The US employs a parallel approach of securities law, commodity law, anti-money laundering, and law enforcement regulation. Mainland China maintains a high-pressure stance against virtual currency trading, token issuance, and illegal financial activities. While the paths differ, the underlying logic remains the same: When digital assets are merely technical credentials, membership benefits, consumption scenarios, or data records, they are primarily technical and commercial issues. However, when digital assets begin to function as investment, trading, generating returns, making payments, and financing, they become financial issues. Financial issues cannot remain in a regulatory vacuum indefinitely. Therefore, when we look at NFTs, RWAs, stablecoins, digital collectibles, on-chain funds, and tokenized assets, we can't just ask whether they're on-chain or what technology they use. The real questions we should ask are: Does it have a fundraising function? Does it have expected returns? Does it have a secondary market? Does it have custodian services for client assets? Does it sell to the public? Does it have payment or settlement functions? Is it possible to turn an ordinary right into a tradable financial product? If the answer is yes, then regulation will come sooner or later. This logic applies to the EU, the US, Hong Kong, Singapore, and mainland China. The difference lies in: some places offer licensing pathways, some draw red lines directly, some enforce regulations before legislating, and some allow pilot programs but restrict boundaries. However, no mature market will allow large-scale financial activities to remain outside of regulation for an extended period. The crypto industry doesn't lack a future, but it lacks a future without licenses. After MiCA, the crypto industry will not disappear. But it will exist in a different form. In the past, the crypto industry liked to talk about technological revolutions, community consensus, decentralized narratives, and wealth stories. In the future, these stories will still exist, but they will no longer be enough. Platforms that truly survive must possess both technological capabilities and institutional adaptability. They must not only be able to write code, but also protect customer assets; they must not only have liquidity, but also a compliance architecture; they must not only be able to expand globally, but also be locally licensed; they must not only talk about innovation, but also assume financial responsibility. This is the biggest reminder the Binance incident has given the industry. Even the world's largest exchanges must face licensing issues; other digital asset projects can no longer afford to imagine circumventing financial regulations with technological narratives. MiCA is not the end for the crypto industry, but rather the beginning of its financialization, licensing, and institutionalization.

data.title: How does monetary policy shape FinTech lending: complementarity or substitution?
data.description:
We used loan-level data from Prosper Marketplace to examine how monetary policy institutions shape FinTech lending and found that FinTech lending behavior exhibits systematic differences across different monetary policy environments and borrower types. During periods of monetary easing, FinTech lending complements traditional banks by offering favorable terms to attract first-time borrowers and providing larger loans to repeat borrowers; conversely, during periods of monetary tightening, the opposite occurs. These findings highlight the dual role of FinTech lending institutions as both complementors and substitutes in changing monetary policy environments. Our findings provide new evidence for the institutionally determined role of FinTech in credit markets and its interaction with borrower heterogeneity and bank structure. The core of this research has been translated by the FinTech Research Institute of Renmin University of China. I. Introduction FinTech lending has transformed credit access by providing fast, flexible, and easily accessible credit, thereby enhancing financial inclusion and reducing borrowing costs for consumers and small businesses. Existing research indicates that refinancing—especially during times of financial difficulty—is a stabilizing tool for households and businesses. While traditional banks face regulatory constraints on refinancing, FinTech platforms operate with greater flexibility and less friction. However, it remains unclear whether FinTech lending institutions systematically offer different terms to specific types of borrowers (e.g., refinancing versus other loan purposes, first-time borrowing versus repeat borrowing), and whether this differential treatment depends on the macroeconomic environment. Another important aspect of FinTech lending is its role in the financial system relative to traditional bank lending institutions. FinTech lending institutions serve both borrowers who can obtain bank loans and those who cannot. The first category of borrowers can choose between bank loans and FinTech loans based on the required amount, convenience, and speed of loan approval. On the other hand, FinTech lending institutions serve as an alternative for borrowers who may be unable to obtain bank loans due to residing in remote areas where banks do not have branches or being deemed to exceed the bank's risk tolerance. The segmentation of borrowers who choose to replace bank loans with FinTech loans varies depending on the macroeconomic environment and policies regulating bank lending. Furthermore, FinTech lenders' preferences for different types of borrowers and their roles relative to traditional bank lenders do not remain constant over time. Instead, they are largely dependent on the macroeconomic environment, which creates different levels of risk-taking between banks and FinTech lenders. This paper examines how different macroeconomic environments affect how FinTech platforms differentiate between borrower types and their roles relative to bank lenders. Our results show that FinTech lenders behave and play significantly differently from bank lenders across different macroeconomic environments. Throughout all sample periods, refinancing borrowers consistently received more favorable loan terms than non-refinancing borrowers, with similarly significant differences between first-time refinancing borrowers and repeat refinancing borrowers. These patterns suggest that loose monetary policy conditions intensify competition for borrowers and stimulate refinancing activity on FinTech platforms. Secondly, we found that FinTech lending institutions complement banks during periods of monetary easing, while substituting for bank loans during periods of monetary tightening. During periods of monetary easing, Prosper attracted relatively higher-quality borrowers, improving the overall creditworthiness of FinTech loans. Furthermore, in regions with higher bank concentration, FinTech loans, particularly among first-time borrowers, were disbursed in higher volumes; however, repeat borrowers remained more resilient, maintaining stronger credit scores and obtaining more favorable terms.

II.Research Questions and Contributions

Our research covers two main areas of inquiry: First, we examine whether FinTech platforms offer different loan terms for refinancing and non-refinancing transactions, whether underlying monetary policy conditions affect this differentiation, and whether repeat borrowers and first-time borrowers are treated differently; Second, we examine whether

Three. Data Source

Our research relies on a comprehensive dataset from Prosper, one of the largest P2P lending platforms in the United States. Prosper offers loans of up to $50,000 for a variety of purposes, including debt refinancing, home improvement, and small business financing. Prosper sets a fixed interest rate that borrowers must agree to before the loan is issued. Investors then bid to fund these loans, and funding is only released when a predetermined commitment threshold is met, thus preventing any negotiation between borrowers and lenders. Prosper allocates priced loan applications to different investment channels and uses a clear mechanism to determine investors' access to and participation in loan financing.
