HHI A higher HHI value indicates higher bank concentration, while a lower HHI value indicates a more competitive banking environment; ?₃ captures the differences in the relationship between bank concentration and FinTech lending between pre-COVID-19 periods and periods of monetary easing and tightening. If FinTech platforms complement traditional banking, we expect FinTech lending activity to be higher in states with lower bank concentration, resulting in a negative ?₃. V. Empirical Results The results of the regression (Equation 1) show that refinancing obtained larger amounts, lower interest rates, and longer terms compared to other borrowers, which is associated with significantly better loan terms, as expected. FinTech platforms offer preferential loan terms to refinanced borrowers, specifically allowing them to obtain larger loan amounts, lower interest rates, and longer terms, with a stronger effect on repeat borrowers. The effect of refinancing also varies with monetary policy regimes. During periods of monetary easing, refinancing is associated with larger loan sizes and lower borrowing costs, particularly for first-time borrowers. During periods of monetary tightening, lending becomes more restrictive for first-time borrowers, while repeat borrowers continue to receive relatively more favorable terms. In conclusion, our findings suggest that… FinTech platforms adjust loan terms across various monetary policy regimes while maintaining more favorable conditions for borrowers with established credit histories. 3. FinTech and Banks: Substitute or Complement? FinTech platforms can act as either complements or substitutes to traditional banks, depending on borrowers' credit accessibility and the current economic environment. When borrowers have broad access to credit, FinTech platforms can be more effective. FinTech platforms supplement bank loans by providing alternative financing channels. In this scenario, there is overlap in the borrower base between banks and FinTech platforms. However, during periods of heightened risk or for borrowers with weaker credit, FinTech platforms may act as substitutes, providing loans that banks are less willing to extend. This leads FinTech platforms to serve higher-risk borrowers, thereby reducing the overlap with banks in terms of borrower base. Conclusion This paper examines how Prosper, a representative FinTech lending platform, adjusts its credit allocation strategy under different monetary policy environments. Through a detailed analysis of loan characteristics and borrower quality during these periods, we find that refinancing borrowers (especially repeat borrowers) consistently receive more favorable financial terms, reflecting their existing credit history and lower risk perception. In contrast, terms for first-time borrowers vary significantly depending on the economic environment of each crisis. During periods of monetary easing, FinTech... FinTech lending supplements traditional banking by attracting higher-quality borrowers and actively participates in states with lower banking market concentration. These patterns suggest that loose monetary conditions enable FinTech lending institutions to compete more effectively with traditional lenders in a market already highly competitive with banks. Conversely, during periods of monetary tightening, FinTech lending increasingly substitutes for traditional banking, particularly in states with higher banking market concentration. As borrowing costs rise and risk aversion intensifies, it becomes more difficult for lower-quality borrowers to obtain loans from traditional banks, leading them to increasingly turn to FinTech. FinTech lending platforms access credit. Furthermore, our findings indicate that repeat borrowers demonstrate greater resilience than first-time borrowers in maintaining borrower quality during periods of monetary tightening, highlighting the platforms' confidence in existing borrowers even under adverse conditions. These findings underscore the evolving role of FinTech lending platforms under different monetary policy regimes. Policymakers should consider frameworks to promote innovation and mitigate potential risks associated with FinTech lending, especially as these platforms increasingly replace traditional banking services during periods of monetary tightening. Regulatory measures could include monitoring credit risk management practices and ensuring FinTech lending institutions maintain robust risk buffers to prevent systemic vulnerabilities. Additionally, promoting… Greater collaboration between FinTech platforms and traditional banks—especially during times of greatest liquidity constraint—could improve credit allocation.

data.title: Binance And Founder CZ Hit By £150 Million Lawsuit For Alleged Fraud As The Exchange Struggles To Get Footing In Europe
data.description:
Binance and its founder, Changpeng "CZ" Zhao, have been hit with a nearly £150 million lawsuit in the United Kingdom, adding to the crypto exchange's growing regulatory challenges across Europe.

Binance and its co-founder Changpeng Zhao face a claim from nearly 1,700 investors who allege that the world’s biggest crypto exchange offered risky derivative products to retail traders without approval from the UK financial regulator. https://t.co/WD2b0a6i0wpic.twitter.com/V2NmzBjLqP

— Financial Times (@FT) June 30, 2026

According to Reuters, almost 1,700 British investors are seeking more than £150 million from the exchange, accusing Binance of offering complex and high-risk leveraged crypto products without the necessary regulatory approval. Some claimants reportedly lost tens of thousands of dollars after trading the products in late 2019.

Although the disputed products were offered in 2019, the UK Financial Conduct Authority later banned crypto derivatives for retail investors in October 2020, arguing that the products were too complex and risky for ordinary consumers. The ban came into force in January 2021.

🚨 UK UNLEASHES LANDMARK CRYPTO RULEBOOK!

FCA JUST FINALIZED MAJOR RULES ON CAPITAL, MARKET ABUSE & STABLECOINS — FULL REGIME HITS OCTOBER 2027! pic.twitter.com/Jov5bxy3D9

— Crypto News Hunters 🎯 (@CryptoNewsHntrs) June 30, 2026

In its statement, Binance expressed that it would defend itself against all the allegations

"Binance remains committed to its obligations to users and to operating in accordance with applicable law."

While the FCA recently relaxed restrictions on retail access to certain crypto exchange-traded notes, it has not changed its stance on crypto derivatives, reiterating this week that cryptoassets remain high-risk investments under its regulatory framework.

Latest setback in Binance's European battle

The lawsuit arrives as Binance faces mounting regulatory pressure elsewhere in Europe.

Just last week, the exchange withdrew its application for a Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) license in Greece after Reuters reported the application was likely to be rejected. Without MiCA authorization, Binance cannot legally offer crypto services across the European Union under the bloc's new regulatory regime.

🇪🇺🇪🇸 SPAIN JUST SLAMMED THE LAST DOOR ON BINANCE

"No exceptions or extensions."

That's Spain's top markets regulator, days after Binance's EU license collapsed.

No waivers past July 1. And because MiCA is EU-wide, losing Spain means losing all 27 countries.

No grace period.… https://t.co/5svfpYdYVppic.twitter.com/IVRIxJ7cGh

— Merlijn The Trader (@MerlijnTrader) June 29, 2026

The company has said it plans to pursue a MiCA license through another EU member state, although it has yet to reveal which jurisdiction it intends to use.

data.title: Why haven't Hong Kong stocks kept pace with the global bull market?
data.description:
By Huang Fan

Hong Kong's stock market has failed to keep pace with the global bull market, and has even become one of the few major markets to decline against the trend. Why is Hong Kong stock market conspicuously absent from the global capital market feast? What is the future path for Hong Kong stocks?

Since the beginning of this year, global stock markets have continued their strong upward trend from 2025. According to Bloomberg data,As ofAs of June 25, Japan's Nikkei 225 index, led by AI hard technology, has risen by more than 40%, while South Korea's KOSPI index has more than doubled this year, and the US Nasdaq 100 index has also risen by about 15%.

The domestic A-share market also showed no weakness. Encouraged by the slogan "Standing in the Light, Remembering in the Chip," domestic institutions and individual investors frantically chased after companies related to "optical modules and AI chips." During the same period, the STAR Market 50 Index rose by over 50%, the ChiNext Index rose by about 35%, and even the CSI 300 Index, representing the overall market, saw a 10% increase. However, the Hong Kong stock market failed to keep pace with the global bull market, even becoming one of the few major markets to decline against the trend. The Hang Seng Index has fallen 10% year-to-date (as of June 25), while the Hang Seng TECH Index (focusing on mainland technology companies listed in Hong Kong) has fallen by as much as 20% during the same period. Clearly, the global capital market feast was conspicuously absent from the Hong Kong stock market. Why is this the case? I. Capital Flows are the Main Driver of Hong Kong Stocks While major global markets have performed strongly driven by the AI ​​boom, Hong Kong stocks have historically been dominated by foreign capital. However, global funds are extremely sensitive to geopolitics and global liquidity, and also anticipate inflation and a relatively tighter monetary policy from the Federal Reserve. In recent years, a significant amount of capital has flowed back from Hong Kong stocks to the US or other surrounding markets. During the same period, the proportion of mainland funds in the Hong Kong market has been increasing. The strong rebound of Hong Kong stocks in 2025 attracted a large influx of mainland funds southbound. According to data from the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission, in the year ending March 2026, net purchases of Hong Kong stocks by southbound funds reached approximately HK$1.19 trillion (approximately US$152 billion), a record high. However, the flow reversed in the second quarter of 2026: mainland investors' southbound funds returned northbound due to the booming domestic science and technology innovation and ChiNext markets. According to Bloomberg data, in May 2026, mainland southbound funds net sold HK$3.6 billion worth of Hong Kong stocks, the first monthly net outflow since June 2023. At the same time, investors withdrew RMB 25 billion from mainland ETFs tracking Hong Kong stocks, reflecting a decline in mainland funds' enthusiasm for Hong Kong stocks. Overall, funds are flowing out of the Hong Kong market. Coupled with the booming IPO market attracting significant capital participation, the Hong Kong secondary market is sluggish due to a lack of capital inflows. II. The Structure of Hong Kong-Listed Companies is Also Key The Hong Kong stock market has a unique industry and sector structure. Currently, the top ten weighted stocks in the Hang Seng Index include large mainland technology companies (Tencent, Alibaba, Meituan, Xiaomi, etc.), financial insurance companies (HSBC, AIA, CCB, etc.), energy companies (CNOOC), and real estate companies (CK Asset, Sun Hung Kai, etc.). This structure has put Hong Kong stocks under pressure from both sides this year: On the one hand, the global market's rise in 2026 is largely driven by the "AI-driven tech stock boom." Hong Kong's tech sector is dominated by internet and consumer technology platforms (such as Tencent, Alibaba, Meituan, and Xiaomi). These companies not only have limited direct benefits from AI hardware or infrastructure, but also have to make significant capital investments to keep up with the AI ​​wave, impacting their current profitability. On the other hand, traditional mainland financial and real estate companies are constrained by the adjustment cycle of the mainland property market and the potential bad debt pressure on financial institutions corresponding to the downward cycle, resulting in undervaluation by the market. Furthermore, a closer look at the composition structure of the Hang Seng TECH Index, which is benchmarked against AI hard technology indices such as the US Nasdaq and the A-share STAR Market 50 Index, reveals the following: As of the end of May 2026, the 30 constituent stocks of the Hang Seng TECH Index come from the following four sectors in the Hang Seng Industry Classification System (HSIC): Industrials, Consumer Discretionary, Healthcare, and Information Technology. Analyzing the weight distribution and number of companies in each sector within the index shows that the Consumer Discretionary sector comprises 16 companies, accounting for approximately 53.9% of the total weight. This sector covers numerous internet platforms, e-commerce companies, automotive companies, and consumer technology companies, making it the largest sector by weight in the Hang Seng TECH Index. The new AI unicorn companies were only officially included in the recent May 2026 quarterly adjustment, with MiniMax and Zhipu, two domestic AI large-scale model companies, being formally included. Although these largest "non-essential consumer" sectors include tech giants like Alibaba, whose cloud computing, chip, and large-scale model technologies are very strong in AI applications, their traditional businesses (such as e-commerce) still account for the majority of their revenue. Recently, these tech giants have become less popular with investors due to slower revenue growth in their traditional businesses and some profits being "eaten up" by huge capital expenditures. These giants have seen a decline in revenue growth in traditional internet-related advertising, e-commerce, and gaming sectors (reflecting a weak consumer recovery); meanwhile, the industry has transitioned from an "expansion phase" to a "competition phase," with "involutionary competition" (such as the subsidy wars in food delivery and e-commerce), significantly increasing operating costs and thus squeezing profit margins. Therefore, the AI ​​technology wave brings these giants a "profit burden, not a profit contribution." Moreover, to avoid falling behind in the AI ​​era, these giants have invested heavily in AI-related areas (computing power, models, chips), but short-term returns are not obvious, instead putting pressure on overall profits. III. Where does the future of Hong Kong stocks lie? In fact, the capital market is never truly lacking in funds, because funds always chase opportunities. Whether investors' confidence in the market and their expectations for listed companies' performance change has become a key factor in whether the Hong Kong stock market can reverse its downward trend. We see that Hong Kong is also trying to boost market confidence, including measures to attract high-quality listed companies to list on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange: the Hong Kong Stock Exchange is implementing listing system reforms, allowing companies with different voting rights structures to list, and is also discussing reducing the stock stamp duty to stimulate trading. If the mainland's macroeconomic policies increase their support for the economy, such as the introduction of specific measures to help residents repair their balance sheets and boost consumption through "more proactive fiscal policies" and "loose monetary policies," it will significantly improve investors' expectations for the overall performance of listed companies. From the perspective of the structure of Hong Kong-listed companies, although Hong Kong-listed technology companies are mainly large internet platform companies, these large platforms are not currently the focus of the AI ​​hard technology market. However, these internet platforms possess a large amount of data and user scenarios, giving them a strong advantage in the AI ​​era. Their investment in AI technology may bring efficiency improvements (such as precise advertising and automated customer service), product innovation (new service models driven by AI), and business model expansion (such as AI-as-a-service output). These are expected to strengthen their ecosystem moat and profitability. If executed properly, these giants will not only avoid being left behind by the times as investors fear, but may also consolidate their leading position. In fact, these former Chinese technology giants are accelerating the implementation of AI models and self-developed chips, expecting to monetize faster and profit from AI earlier. In addition, the Hang Seng TECH Index also includes many hard technology/semiconductor companies, such as SMIC, Hua Hong Semiconductor, and SenseTime, which are semiconductor manufacturing and AI core technology companies. The AI ​​era has the most direct impact on semiconductor and hard technology companies. Generative AI model training and inference have triggered a surge in global computing power demand, with global semiconductor sales soaring to US$791.7 billion in 2025, a year-on-year increase of 25.6%. This has led to a shortage of GPUs, AI chips, and advanced process foundry capacity, becoming a major boon for hard technology companies. Domestically, under geopolitical pressure, the government is strengthening its "self-reliance and control," and plans to establish a US$40 billion chip fund to support the domestic semiconductor supply chain. Therefore, the AI ​​wave has significantly improved the demand prospects for mainland chip manufacturers: SMIC's revenue is expected to grow by 16.2% in 2025, with net profit increasing by 39%; capacity utilization will rise to 93.5%, and prices will be raised by 10% to cope with the AI ​​hardware boom. The increasing number of domestic technology companies listing in Hong Kong and their gradual inclusion in the Hang Seng TECH Index have driven structural changes in the Hang Seng Index. In recent years, the Hang Seng Index has underperformed, with valuations as low as 20 times earnings, a significant advantage compared to the 160 times earnings of the A-share STAR Market. Overall, Hong Kong stocks are currently undervalued in the global capital market. The Hang Seng Index's current static price-to-earnings ratio is only about 11 times, roughly half that of the S&P 500. The Hang Seng Index's dividend yield is over 3.0%, far exceeding the approximately 1% dividend yield of the US stock market. High cost-effectiveness is the key to potentially high future returns. In the near future, if the profits of domestic internet giants resume growth, the mainland real estate market gradually stabilizes, non-performing assets of financial institutions decline, and the profitability of the new energy sector rebounds, it is expected to improve the overall earnings expectations of global investors for Hong Kong-listed companies, thereby driving valuation increases. From a longer-term perspective, Hong Kong stocks have the potential for structural revaluation. Historical experience shows that after a period of stagnation, the Hong Kong market eventually returns to its growth trend. Hong Kong stocks played a similar role to the current situation in 1998, 2003, 2008, 2015, and 2022, consistently ranking at the bottom of the global market, but subsequently experienced strong rebounds. For example, the Hang Seng Index hit a 15-year low of 14,687 in October 2022, jokingly referred to by investors as the "ruin of the international financial center." However, it subsequently rebounded strongly by over 50% in 2024-2025. The upgrading of China's economic structure is a highly probable future trend (high technology, release of consumption potential), and Hong Kong's market mechanisms and structures are also undergoing reform and improvement (enhancing governance, attracting new economy companies to list, etc.). These long-term favorable factors are expected to restore global investor confidence. Compared with other major global markets and even with domestic A-shares, Hong Kong stocks currently have a significant valuation discount. Once the aforementioned long-term favorable factors are fully realized, they are expected to receive a dual boost from valuation increases and profit growth. Therefore, from a 3-5 year or even longer perspective, Hong Kong stocks do indeed possess high investment value. I firmly believe that the historical law of "things reaching their extremes will eventually reverse" will come into play, and it's only a matter of time before the Hong Kong stock market attracts mainland funds back to the south. It's also something to look forward to seeing Hong Kong investors rejoin the feast of technology assets.

data.title: In two years, he "chatted" with AI and started a smart ring company that turned a profit.
data.description:
Author｜Su ZihuaEditor｜Jingyu

0 full-time employees, creating a blockbuster product.

This was originally an ordinary startup story that could easily be overlooked.

In 2025, a smart ring called AIVELA Ring Pro on Kickstarter raised approximately $860,000. In today's market, where projects often raise tens or hundreds of millions of dollars, this figure is not particularly impressive.

But what if this was a project launched solo, with no full-time employees, even the founder working part-time, and yet still profitable? Wouldn't that be different? And what if the founder was originally a "novice" in the smart ring field, but spent their after-get off work hours directing AI to conduct competitive analysis, product design, project management, etc., ultimately achieving a breakthrough in quality within 14 months, launching a high-barrier-to-entry product with the strongest product capabilities in the industry, already in mass production and selling? Wouldn't that be even more remarkable? AIVELA founder Li Donghao graduated from Peking University's Yuanpei Program and holds a master's degree from Cornell University, with a background in biology. He was previously the co-founder of the electric bicycle company Urtopia. He told GeekPark that the reason he decided to solo the smart ring project was "purely a personal hobby, and I didn't want to spend company money." So, in May or June of 2024, Li Donghao started the project alone in his spare time. This April, Li Donghao decided to leave Urtopia and establish a new company to focus on developing the AIVELA brand.

AIVELA's first-generation product, the AIVELA Ring Pro, the world's first interactive smart ring | Image source: AIVELA official website

There are two main reasons. One is more commercial: he personally felt that the smart ring category has "crossed the chasm," from the early geek market, it has begun to rush towards the mass market. ... already "crossed the chasm," from the early geek market, it has begun to rush towards the mass market.

data.title: Thailand Moves Closer To Launching A 1:1 Baht-Pegged Stablecoin Under Central Bank Oversight
data.description:
Thailand is preparing to bring stablecoins under a formal regulatory framework, with plans for a digital token backed by the Thai baht and designed primarily for payments and settlements rather than speculation.

Thailand Moves Closer To A Baht Backed Stablecoin

Bank of Thailand Governor Vitai Ratanakorn said the central bank's study on a baht-pegged stablecoin is nearing completion, paving the way for the country's first regulated stablecoin framework.

Speaking at a financial conference hosted by efinanceThai on 30 June, Ratanakorn said the central bank plans to open a public consultation on the proposal by the end of the year.

Formal regulations are expected to follow in late 2026 or early 2027.

The initiative forms part of Thailand's wider push to support financial innovation while keeping strict oversight of consumer protection and domestic money flows.

Bank Of Thailand Plans Baht Stablecoin

Bank of Thailand plans to introduce a 1:1 baht-backed stablecoin, with a public hearing planned before year-end, Governor Vitai Ratanakorn said on June 30.

The stablecoin would require full baht reserves and initially support institutional… pic.twitter.com/jEzdwNRiUN

— BSCN (@BSCNews) July 1, 2026

How Will The Stablecoin Be Backed

Under the proposed rules, every stablecoin issued must be fully backed on a one-to-one basis with Thai baht reserves.

The funds would be held in segregated accounts at licensed financial institutions to ensure that each digital token is supported by an equivalent amount of fiat currency.

The central bank intends to take a cautious approach.

In its first phase, the stablecoin will only be used by financial institutions for payment settlements, with broader applications to be considered later.

The Bank of Thailand has also indicated that the technology could eventually be used in areas such as carbon credit markets and green finance, where programmable payments may help automate transactions and compliance processes.

A Payments Tool Rather Than A Speculative Asset

Thailand's central bank has made clear that it views stablecoins primarily as payment infrastructure instead of investment products.

The groundwork for the initiative began in 2024 with the launch of the Programmable Payment Sandbox, a controlled environment created to test Thai baht-backed digital payment instruments.

🇹🇭 Thailand is launching a sandbox to allow foreign tourists to convert digital assets into Baht via licensed platforms.

Once converted, the funds can be used through regulated e-money apps, enabling QR code payments and other digital transactions. pic.twitter.com/wzPplOj5ai

— Cryptic (@Cryptic_Web3) July 17, 2025

The sandbox was expanded on 24 December 2025 to allow wider experimentation with the technology.

Ratanakorn's latest comments suggest the central bank remains focused on developing practical uses for stablecoins while limiting risks linked to speculative activity.

Authorities Tighten Rules On Foreign Currency Payments

At the same time, the Bank of Thailand is stepping up enforcement against unauthorised foreign currency transactions.

Ratanakorn reiterated that all personal QR code payments in Thailand must be conducted exclusively in Thai baht.

Regulators have already suspended around 5,000 accounts that were used for peer-to-peer yuan transfers through Alipay and WeChat Pay between February 2025 and May 2026.

The Bank of Thailand (BOT) is intensively elevating its supervision of the financial system.

This follows a previous disclosure by Chayawadee Chai-Anant, Assistant Governor, Corporate Relations Group, that the BOT had coordinated with Alipay and WeChat Pay providers to suspend… pic.twitter.com/nMi1xbugAl

— Thenationthailand (@Thenationth) June 26, 2026

The central bank is now working with both payment platforms to review transactions and identify possible regulatory breaches.

Heavy Penalties For Unauthorised Forex Activities

Payment providers that process transactions in unauthorised currencies could face corrective measures, financial penalties, licence suspensions or even the revocation of their operating permits.

Ratanakorn also said the central bank will not issue licences for retail foreign exchange services intended for speculative trading.

Under Thailand's Exchange Control Act of 1942, facilitating transfers linked to speculative forex activities can result in penalties of up to three years in prison and fines of 200,000 baht, or around $6,012.

Individuals who advertise or promote speculative currency trading may also face fraud charges under a 1984 emergency decree, carrying penalties of up to 10 years' imprisonment and substantial daily fines.

Ratanakorn said the Bank of Thailand's dual objective is to foster financial technology while maintaining strict control over consumer protection and domestic currency flows.

data.title: Market Notes: Stablecoin Alliance OUSD Sieges Circle
data.description:
On June 30th, over 140 institutions, including Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, Coinbase, BlackRock, BNY Mellon, Google, and Shopify, jointly announced the formation of the stablecoin alliance "Open USD" (OUSD). It features no minting or redemption fees, no trading volume cap, and reserve profits, after deducting management fees, are returned to participating parties, not exclusively held by any single issuer. Tether and Circle were not invited. The question in May was "Should banks issue their own tokens?" June offered another answer: They don't have to issue their own tokens—as long as they unite and effectively sideline the issuers. This was the most important event of the month, but not the only one. On June 11th, tokenized infrastructure provider Digital Asset (parent company of Canton Network) completed a $355 million funding round. The investor list included ABN Amro, BNP Paribas, HSBC, Citadel Securities, S&P Global, Tradeweb, CME Ventures, SoFi, Apollo, and the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority – the most crowded joint bet by TradeFi institutions on a single tokenized infrastructure to date. On June 10th, Visa and Mastercard launched their major agentic payments products on the same day, a direct response to the cloud giants' offensive in May. The three issues pertain to stablecoin payments, on-chain finance, and agentic commerce, but share a common direction: May focused on "building the pipeline," while June focuses on "who decides who owns the pipeline after it's built." This issue continues to cover these three areas: stablecoin payments, on-chain finance, and agentic commerce, plus the fundraising section. Below is the complete picture for June. June Market Snapshot The total supply of stablecoins is approximately $314-322 billion, roughly the same as in May, marking the first plateau after several months of record highs.

I. Stablecoin Payment

Main theme: The question in May was whether banks should issue their own coins. The answer in June was—they don't have to issue coins, as long as they unite to sideline the issuers.

Main theme: The question in May was whether banks should issue their own coins. The answer in June was—they don't have to issue coins, as long as they unite to sideline the issuers.

Main theme: The question in May was whether banks should issue their own coins. The answer in June was—they don't have to issue coins, as long as they unite to sideline the issuers.

1.1 Open USD: A Joint Coin Minting Effort by 140 Companies

On June 30, the "Open Standard" consortium announced Open USD (OUSD). Its core mechanism is completely different from USDT/USDC: minting and redemption are free of charge, there is no trading volume cap, and most of the reserve revenue, after deducting management fees, is returned to the participants, not exclusively held by a single issuer. It is planned to be deployed on multiple chains including Base/Ethereum/Solana, and may be natively implemented on Tempo, with an expected launch "later this year". Bridge co-founder Zach Abrams has been appointed as interim CEO.

Tether and Circle are not involved.

Circle's stock price plummeted 13-17% that day. Comment: OUSD is not a no-man's-land; there is a clear mastermind behind these 140 names. Zach Abrams, the founding CEO of Open Standard, is also the co-founder of Bridge—Bridge is the stablecoin infrastructure company acquired by Stripe for $1.1 billion, which comes with its own reserve management (BlackRock/Fidelity/Superstate custody) and "issuance + revenue sharing" product logic. The core selling point of OUSD—no minting/redemption fees and reserve revenue sharing with participants—is essentially a large-scale replication of the Open Issuance product logic under Bridge, only upgrading the service target from a single enterprise client to 140 giants sharing the same coin. In terms of wording, Stripe is the only partner to use the term "default"—"OUSD will become the default stablecoin for businesses on the Stripe platform." This is a concrete commitment to driving traffic to existing businesses. Visa says it will "bring risk control standards," and Mastercard says "infrastructure should be open," both remaining at the level of value declarations. Abrams' title is still "interim CEO," and the governance structure has not yet been finalized, which in itself is the first signal. The governance structure that OUSD wants to build is precisely the one that Visa itself spent nearly fifty years getting rid of; Rome wasn't built in a day. Visa's network of over 20,000 banks wasn't designed in the first place. It was the result of decades of evolution, starting with BankAmericard in 1958, following the old path of a "members' association" with constant debate and changes, until 2007 when it finally took back governance from its member banks and became a publicly traded company with an independent board of directors, finally making it truly operational. Moreover, this path wasn't exactly honorable; the structure of "member banks being both owners and competitors" was precisely the core target of Visa/Mastercard's merchant antitrust lawsuits from the 1990s to the 2000s. The most ironic part is that Visa and Mastercard themselves are co-signers of OUSD. They know better than anyone what the end result will look like, yet they still signed—this either indicates they don't have high expectations for OUSD governance and are just occupying a position, or it means they're betting "this time will be different." Both possibilities deserve further observation to see which one comes true. The chain of interests also needs to be analyzed, but we can't simply apply the model of credit card organizations here. This is the core; otherwise, it will fall apart. The core mechanism of OUSD is to change the floating returns generated by stablecoin reserve assets (mainly US Treasury bonds) from being received by the issuer alone to being distributed among 140 participants based on transaction volume. This logic is often compared to the interchange of card organizations, but their asset structures are actually different. Card organizations' money is easy to distribute because revenue is naturally transaction-by-transaction: for each transaction, the merchant deducts MOR, the issuing bank receives interchange, and the card organization receives network fees. Whoever processes the transaction receives the money; the accounts are clean, auditable, and disputes are easy to resolve. Stablecoin reserve returns are not structured this way; they are pooled—140 participants share the same reserve pool, and the returns are not generated by "who processed this transaction," but by the "entire OUSD circulating supply." This raises a question that the official announcement completely failed to answer: What is the allocation based on? Is it based on the proportion of OUSD reserves minted/distributed by each entity? On the trading volume generated by each channel? Or is it a fixed, negotiated ratio unrelated to activity levels? To date, no party has publicly disclosed this allocation formula, making this the most crucial information gap to monitor – and once this formula is made public, it will most likely be the first target of scrutiny under the OCC's "related party benefit anti-circumvention presumption" clause. The most noteworthy aspect here is the conflict between two key figures: Coinbase is both Circle's largest distributor (annual fee of $908 million) and a co-signatory of OUSD. The upcoming renewal of Coinbase and Circle's distribution agreement in August is the first verifiable external signal. BlackRock manages Circle's USDC reserve fund and is also one of the initiators of OUSD—betting on both sides suggests that even the reserve manager itself is unsure who will win. The number 140+ itself also warrants scrutiny—the real issue isn't the length of the list, but whether the word "participation" carries any real weight. Having an "OUSD" currency option added to a bank's ledger is entirely different from the bank actually using OUSD as its default settlement asset, channeling actual trading volume to it, and committing to a certain percentage of reserves. The former is simply an IT department adding a line of code; the latter is a genuine business commitment. Apart from Stripe, which has explicitly stated it as "default," the remaining 139 banks are currently only providing perfunctory statements in press releases—"list depth" is more worthy of detailed description than "list length." The controversy surrounding governance currently involves two contradictory suspicions. Analysts like CoinLaw are concerned about "oligopoly"—whether the dominant players among the 140 partners (especially Stripe/Visa/Mastercard) will exert disproportionate influence on the network's direction in controversial decisions. Lorenzo Valente of Ark Invest offered a completely opposite perspective: he preferred to bet on two operators capable of making unilateral decisions rather than a committee that had to consult with 140 shareholders, arguing that such a bloated governance structure would leave no one in control and would be less efficient than the single-issuer systems like Circle and Tether. Valente added a more straightforward calculation: If Open Standard were to follow the usual practice of other consortiums and take 25 basis points, even with a supply of $10 billion, it would only earn $25 million a year—a sum insufficient to support Circle's comprehensive partner system encompassing on/off ramps, OTC platforms, and settlement rebates. "A consortium that shares profits" and "an issuer that spends money to nurture its ecosystem" are fundamentally two different business models, and OUSD's current announcement only addresses the former. These two suspicions are contradictory, indicating that the outside world currently cannot fully understand OUSD's true internal power structure; this can only be verified when it encounters its first controversial decision after launch. Historical precedent provides a less-than-optimistic benchmark. Diem (formerly Libra) died under regulatory pressure as its partners withdrew one by one—the strength of an alliance depends on the weakest exit cost, not the strongest willingness to join. USDG (Global Dollar Network, owned by Paxos) survived, but after three years of operation, its supply only reached about $3 billion, lagging behind USDT/USDC by dozens of times—this shows that "not dead but not successful" is the default fate of alliance-type stablecoins, and there is no need for regulatory intervention to kill them. Several verifiable signals that are truly worth watching: Who voted on the change in reserve asset allocation, when will the allocation formula be made public, whether the Coinbase-Circle renewal terms in August were improved due to OUSD, when Abrams' "interim" will be officially appointed and who will appoint him, and how many of the 140 partners have actually integrated OUSD into their settlement transactions after 90 days of operation rather than just displaying a logo—what we can see now is just an announcement and a board of directors list; governance is forged through real conflicts of interest, not through joint statements. So, as the teacher in the group said: Ultimately, the biggest risk of such large-scale alliances isn't technology, but formalism—with such a massive setup, many practical aspects may not be properly streamlined, ultimately turning into a joke. Bitcoin has long proven this system unnecessary: ​​the protocol works, nodes are on the platform, whoever wants to connect can connect—no meetings, no signatures from 140 companies, no boardroom photos. The current state of OUSD doesn't resemble Bitcoin; it resembles a group of "old guard" thinking they're doing something big—suits, photos, joint statements. This is also where the K-shaped polarization in the crypto world is most evident: what the older generation considers respectable and solemn, the younger generation sees as ridiculous. The more people in suits, the more reliable the matter is.

1.2 MiCA Deadline Arrives: This Time It's Not Just Tether That's Stuck, But Binance Itself

July 1st is the hard deadline for the MiCA transition period to expire across the EU. ESMA has confirmed that there will be no further extensions—of the more than 1,200 crypto companies that originally held national licenses in various countries, only about 210 (a conversion rate of about 17%) have completed the CASP full license certification. ...1.2 MiCA Deadline Arrives: This Time It's Not Just Tether That's Stuck, But Binance Itself

1.2 Mi USDT has long been excluded from this timeline: major exchanges such as Coinbase, Kraken, and Crypto.com have been delisting USDT from EU users since the end of 2024 to 2025. Tether has not yet applied for EMT authorization under MiCA, citing its refusal to accept the EU requirement that 60% of its reserves be held in European bank deposits. The truly noteworthy new variable in June is that Binance, the world's largest exchange, is also stuck on this line—although Greek regulators have deemed its application materials compliant, the approval process is stalled at the ESMA review stage. Binance has promised to announce a response plan for EU users by June 30th. This means that after July 1st, what will be blocked is not just a non-compliant stablecoin, but potentially the largest trading platform in the industry itself.

1.3 Western Union USDPT Consumer Products Launched in June

Western Union, a giant in cross-border remittances, launched its stablecoin USDPT in June in markets such as Mexico and the Philippines. This marks another milestone following its announcement in April and official release in May. Western Union is also developing a Digital Asset Network for withdrawals from crypto wallets, and a Stable Card that allows users to hold stablecoins and make purchases through card organizations. The fact that a 175-year-old remittance company is embedding stablecoins into its back-end settlement system, rather than packaging them as a new narrative, signals that "stablecoins are becoming a cross-border payment infrastructure rather than just a narrative."

II. On-Chain Finance

    Main Theme: Canton Network's first round of financing brought TradFi's intention to jointly bet on the same infrastructure to the forefront.

    2.1 Data: Tokenized Stock Trading Volume Hits Record High

    On June 24, the daily trading volume of tokenized stocks hit a record high of $565 million, with Solana accounting for 97.8% of the share ($553.3 million). The total monthly volume in June exceeded $6 billion, a 70% increase month-over-month, the highest monthly volume on record.

    Solana tokenized stock trading volume reached $4.9 billion in the first half of the year, a six-fold increase from $775 million in the second half of 2025. Catalysts included the SpaceX IPO and Micron's earnings report, which drove traders to shift towards tokenized exposure. 2.2 Canton Network: The Most Crowded Investment List in TradeFi History On June 11, Digital Asset completed a $355 million funding round, led by a16z crypto, valuing the company at $2 billion, exceeding the original $300 million target. Investors include: ABN Amro, BNP Paribas, HSBC, Citadel Securities, S&P Global, Tradeweb, Broadridge, Optiver, CME Ventures, SBI Group, SoFi, Apollo Funds, Abu Dhabi Investment Authority, Coinbase Ventures, and Polychain. It's worth noting that Visa's involvement with Canton didn't begin with this funding round—Visa joined the Canton network as a Super Validator in March 2026, subsequently incorporating Canton into its stablecoin settlement pilot program. Beyond funding, Canton currently boasts over 700 ecosystem participants, a testament to the scale behind this "most crowded list." 2.3 DTCC: Multi-Chain Strategy Extension On May 27th, DTCC announced that its tokenization service will connect to the Stellar public chain, aiming for a launch in the first half of 2027, as part of its multi-chain strategy in addition to the full launch in October. According to secondary sources (Bitget/Gate), the July pilot may be a one-day test on July 13th, but this has not yet been confirmed by DTCC officials or Reuters/Bloomberg. 2.4 Supply-Side Integration: SEC Still Stuck, Bullish Obtains New License Following the SEC's withdrawal of the exemption for tokenized stock innovation due to opposition from Nasdaq, NYSE, and Cboe on May 22, the framework was still being drafted in June, with no new version officially released. On June 29, Bullish received approval from the Gibraltar Financial Services Commission to conduct tokenized securities trading—a separate development from the Equiniti acquisition (expected to be completed in January 2027). 2.5 Banks Join the Fray: JPMorgan, Citi, BofA, and Wells Fargo Plan Joint Tokenized Deposit Network for 2027 According to a June 5 report in the Wall Street Journal, major U.S. commercial banks, including JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup, Bank of America, and Wells Fargo, plan to launch a joint tokenized deposit network in the first half of 2027, operated by The Clearing House, which is jointly owned by these banks. Internally, the banks refer to this project as "the bridge" or "the chain," aiming to enable 24/7 instant transfer of traditional bank deposits in the form of on-chain tokens, while keeping funds within the regulated banking system. The underlying blockchain technology provider has not yet been selected. Mark Monaco, head of global payments at Bank of America, acknowledged that customers aren't currently "eager" to tokenize deposits, but banks need to position themselves in advance to avoid being caught off guard when demand actually picks up. Canton's funding round differs from Circle Arc's funding round in May. Circle Arc involved NYSE, BlackRock, and Apollo acquiring equity in a chain controlled by Circle; Canton's round involves banks, market makers, sovereign wealth funds, and the Cypto native fund simultaneously acquiring equity in the same protocol—and this protocol itself doesn't belong to any single issuer. The key difference lies in the fact that May's capital activity was about "positioning themselves in a single node," while June's Canton funding round reflected a "concentration of institutional consensus towards a single infrastructure." This indicates that TradFi is no longer content with diversifying its bets across multiple chains and is beginning to form a shared judgment on "which chain will win." The tokenized deposit network of JPMorgan Chase, Citibank, and Bank of America represents another "coalition" path that emerged in the same month—banks are not siding with any particular public chain but are instead tokenizing their deposit business and forming a united front for defense. Essentially, like OUSD and Canton, this is a variation of the judgment that "the window for going it alone is closing." However, the weight of the funding list does not equate to technological consensus. In a report this February, TD Securities analyst Lance Vitanza questioned whether Canton truly qualifies as a blockchain, arguing that many experts consider it closer to "a packaged database in the cloud," failing to meet the open, trustless, and permissionless architecture of Bitcoin. This dissenting opinion is worth mentioning here as a counterpoint: the substantial investments from banks, market makers, and sovereign wealth funds demonstrate a "willingness to bet," not that the "technical approach has proven successful." The record-breaking trading volume of tokenized stocks is undeniable, as is Solana's 97.8% market share. However, there is currently no single, definitive source to confirm the driving factors—SpaceX's IPO and Micron's financial report are frequently cited catalysts, and the six-fold growth curve in the first half of the year may simply be a low base effect. While supply-side financing and licensing figures are easily verifiable, the "why is it rising?" explanation for demand-side increases is often unverifiable; this is a crucial point to consider when analyzing such data. The continued stalling of SEC exemptions indicates that compliance bottlenecks on the supply side have not yet been resolved; Bullish's Gibraltar license is yet another example of circumventing the rules.     3.4 Adyen Launches Adyen Agentic

    In June, Adyen launched Adyen Agentic, a modular API suite covering product information flow, shopping cart creation, and cross-conversational business payment scenarios. This is another example of a payment processor officially packaging its "Agent-oriented product line" as an independent product, consistent with the actions of Visa/Mastercard/Ant Financial. 3.5 Real-world testing: Numerous product releases, almost zero data adoption. Robinhood AI Agent Credit Card (launched May 27), Mastercard AP4M, and AWS AgentCore Payments (previewed May 7) – as of the end of June, none of these three had disclosed any user numbers, transaction counts, or transaction amounts. AI-generated fake expense vouchers accounted for 71% of those flagged as fraudulent (data as of mid-May). The UK FCA CEO delivered a speech on June 24 titled "Rethinking regulation for the age of AI," continuing a forward-looking stance, but no new rules have been implemented. The assessment in May was that "AWS and Google made their moves in the same week, shifting the focus from protocol wars to execution-level control wars." The contrast in June was "Visa and Mastercard retaliated on the same day"—a direct response from card networks to the cloud giants' May offensive, indicating that the battle had expanded from the protocol layer to the entire payment industry chain: cloud platforms, card networks, LLM routing providers, and Chinese payment giants were all vying for a central position in the agentic economy within the same month. The actions of Ant International and Adyen demonstrate that this was not just a war between American companies—almost all major global payment infrastructure providers completed their "Agent product lines" in June. But a month later, Robinhood, Mastercard, and AWS are all focusing on "product release coverage" rather than "data adoption coverage"—this is not a coincidence, but the only honest reality in this market right now: the speed of infrastructure construction far exceeds the actual usage scale that anyone is willing to disclose. The x402 surpassing 100 million transactions is a cumulative figure; cumulative figures don't lie, but they also don't tell you whether growth is slowing. The 77% monthly decline in May is there for all to see, and the delayed release of June's figures is itself a signal. In contrast, Alipay AI Pay's willingness to disclose specific figures like "300 million transactions" is one of the few exceptions this month. IV. June Funding Rounds Main Theme: A funding round and a list of investors explain what TradFi is currently looking to buy. The list of investors in Canton's latest funding round spans five completely different types of capital: banks (ABN Amro, BNP Paribas, HSBC), market makers (Citadel Securities, Optiver), market infrastructure providers (S&P Global, Tradeweb, Broadridge), asset management companies (SBI, SoFi, Apollo), and sovereign wealth funds (Abu Dhabi Investment Authority). All within the same round, with the same valuation and the same agreement. This is unprecedented in the history of tokenized fundraising over the past 18 months. While the investor composition in Circle Arc's May round was already considered diverse, it didn't involve banks, market makers, and sovereign wealth funds all appearing together. Ramp's $750 million and SBI's acquisition of Bitbank represent two continuations of different logics: the former is an escalation of the "agentic economic application layer" narrative from May, while the latter is a continuation of the consolidation of Asian exchanges. Keyrock's acquisition of the bankrupt BlockFills for a mere $3.25 million, far smaller than Drift's $147.5 million bailout in April, indicates that this round of asset restructuring is more measured than April's—not an emergency injection, but a low-price takeover. Official data on total funding in June has not yet been fully released (trackers typically lag by 2-3 weeks), but weekly data shows that funding is characterized by "single, large-scale deals" rather than "overall recovery"—the Canton round raised $130 million in a single week from June 8-14, but fell back to $86.3 million from June 14-20, indicating that the market is still driven by a few super-large deals, and the breadth of funding has not kept up. V. Summary: What the June Market Tells Us The three main themes each underwent a turning point in June—moving from the "construction period" of May to the "confrontation period" of June. In short: the question in May was "who will make this money?", and the answer in June is—the way to make money is shifting from "who controls the issuance" to "who can unite to redistribute it." OUSD, Canton, traditional banks' tokenized deposit networks, and Visa-Mastercard all entered the fray on the same day, representing four different approaches to collaboration, all pointing to the same conclusion: the window for going it alone is closing.
